fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the auth framework work we've been discussing. The basic idea is that app developers shouldn't have to hand-roll all the account flow stuff — sign in, sign out, registration, the whole nine yards. We talked about this being similar to what we did on that other project with the scope-based session handling, so just follow that same pattern for the multi-scope parts.

A few things I want to make sure are covered: users have been complaining that when they type their email in a different case it sometimes doesn't log them in — that needs to be handled consistently. Also we got a bug report that after a password reset the user isn't being signed in automatically, which is frustrating UX. The locked account flow and the confirmation email flow should both support both the regular HTML path and also the JSON API path since some of our consumers are mobile clients.

The remember-me cookie behavior needs to work properly — right now it's not clear if a bad/expired remember token just silently fails or actually redirects. And the session expiry stuff needs to respect whether we're kicking out just one scope or all scopes at once, same logic as the sign-out scope policy. Tracking sign-ins (count, timestamps, IP) should also be suppressible. Can someone make sure all of this is specced out properly before we start? Thanks

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Email (and whitespace) normalization is configurable per account scope. When normalization is enabled, 'Foo@Bar.com' stored and 'foo@bar.com' submitted should authenticate successfully (status=200, authenticated). When normalization is disabled, mismatched case must fail authentication and return the invalid-credentials message. Both case normalization and whitespace stripping are independent toggles that apply to the identifier field.

trigger_keywords: signing out one account affects other
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Sign-out has a configurable boolean policy called 'sign_out_all_account_scopes'. When false, signing out the 'user' scope leaves the 'admin' scope still authenticated (authenticated_admin=true). When true, signing out any single scope terminates all active account scopes. The response is always a 302 redirect, typically to '/'.

After a valid password reset token is consumed with matching password and password_confirmation fields, the framework automatically signs the user in. The response is status=200, path=/, authenticated_user=true, and the message is 'Your password has been changed successfully. You are now signed in.' The stored password is updated (password_changed=true). Invalid tokens or mismatched confirmation fields must leave the password unchanged.

Both the email confirmation flow (feature 8) and the account unlock flow (feature 9) support a JSON format mode in addition to the standard HTML path. The JSON response must include a 'json_has_errors' signal. For a valid instruction request in HTML mode, status=200, path=/users/sign_in, mail_count=1, and json_has_errors=false. Token consumption with an expired or invalid token in JSON mode must return JSON error signals rather than an HTML redirect.

When a request carries an invalid or expired remembered-session token, the framework must redirect to sign-in and leave the user unauthenticated (authenticated_user=false, authenticated_admin=false). A valid remembered token must authenticate the user without an explicit sign-in. When remember=false at sign-in, no remember cookie is created (remember_cookie_present=false). When remember=true, a remember cookie is created (remember_cookie_present=true).

trigger_keywords: expired session which scopes signed out
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Session timeout respects a cross-scope timeout policy analogous to the sign-out scope policy. When a session is expired and the policy applies to all scopes, all scopes are signed out. When not expired (expired=false), the session remains authenticated (status=200, authenticated_user=true). When expired, a redirect_to is returned and authenticated scopes reflect the signed-out state. The visible message field is included in the output.

Sign-in tracking records sign_in_count, timestamps (current_after_last ordering), and IP metadata. Tracking can be explicitly suppressed via a skip signal, in which case the output must indicate skipped-tracking status rather than updated counters. The tracking mode input determines which subset of tracking data is printed: 'count' prints sign_in_count and timestamps_order, IP mode prints IP metadata fields.

trigger_keywords: where does registration redirect after sign up
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The post-registration redirect path is scope-dependent. For the 'admin' account scope, successful registration redirects to /admin_area/home (status=200, authenticated_admin=true). For a 'user' account scope with confirmation required, the account is created but not authenticated and remains unconfirmed. For invalid registration data, the framework returns validation errors, no account is created, and authentication state remains false for both scopes.

trigger_keywords: who sends the reset instructions email
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The password reset instruction email is sent from 'please-change-me@config-initializers-devise.com' (the default unconfigured sender address). The mail_to field reflects the submitted (normalized) email address, not the stored one. The email body must contain a reset link (body_has_reset_path=true). mail_count=1 for a matched account, and the response path after submitting the forgot-password form is /users/sign_in.

trigger_keywords: redirect when not signed in admin
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Accessing a protected administrator area without any session returns status=302, path=null, redirect_to=/admin_area/sign_in, authenticated_user=false, authenticated_admin=false, body_marker=none. Accessing with a regular user session also redirects (insufficient scope). Only an active admin-scope session returns status=200 with the protected body_marker present.

Account updates require the current password to be correct. If the current password is wrong, the persisted account data (email, password) must remain unchanged. A successful update with a new email returns status=200, path=/, authenticated_user=true, message='Your account has been updated successfully.', and stored_email reflects the new value. If a new password is also submitted, a password_valid signal is included in the output.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The cross-scope session policy logic used in feature3_sign_out_scope_policy — specifically the 'sign_out_all_account_scopes' boolean flag — which must be mirrored in the session timeout scope policy (feature 11) and the multi-scope sign-in/sign-out flows.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same pattern for the multi-scope parts
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The dual-scope authentication model where 'user' and 'admin' are independent account scopes, each tracked separately via authenticated_user and authenticated_admin flags, with independent sign-in, sign-out, timeout, and route protection behaviors as defined across features 1–3 and 11.

feature10_remembered_sessions.json token-consumption behavior: an invalid remembered token must produce a redirect to sign-in (not a silent 200 with unauthenticated state), and remember_cookie_present must be reported in all remember-session mode outputs.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I have full visibility into from the business side — you'd want to dig into the test case contracts and the PRD feature descriptions to get the exact expected behavior. The core intent is that each auth flow should behave consistently and predictably for the developer integrating the framework, so look at what signals each scenario is expected to emit and work backward from there.

feature10_remembered_sessions.json.description: Remember-me sessions create cookies when requested and authenticate future requests only with a valid remembered token.

feature11_session_timeout.json.description: Expired sessions are signed out according to scope policy while active sessions remain authenticated.

feature12_sign_in_tracking.json.description: Successful sign-ins update tracking counters and IP metadata unless tracking is explicitly skipped.

feature1_session_sign_in.json.description: Session sign-in authenticates the correct account scope, applies configured identifier normalization, and reports HTTP/session signals.

feature2_protected_routes.json.description: Protected routes allow only the required authenticated scope and expose redirect or success signals.

cases.expected_output:
status=302
path=null
redirect_to=/admin_area/sign_in
authenticated_user=false
authenticated_admin=false
body_marker=none

feature3_sign_out_scope_policy.json.description: Signing out one account scope respects the configured cross-scope sign-out policy.

feature4_registration.json.description: Registration creates accounts, authenticates active accounts, and blocks inactive accounts pending confirmation.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
path=/admin_area/home
authenticated_user=false
authenticated_admin=true
message=Welcome! You have signed up successfully.
created_email=new_user@test.com

feature5_account_update.json.description: Authenticated account updates require the current password and preserve or replace credentials according to submitted fields.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
path=/
authenticated_user=true
authenticated_admin=false
message=Your account has been updated successfully.
stored_email=user.new@example.com

feature6_password_reset_request.json.description: Password reset requests normalize configured identifiers, return framework responses, and send reset instructions only for matched accounts.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
path=/users/sign_in
message=You will receive an email with instructions on how to reset your password in a few minutes.
mail_count=1
mail_to=foo@bar.com
mail_from=please-change-me@config-initializers-devise.com
body_has_reset_path=true

feature7_password_reset_change.json.description: Password reset token consumption changes the password only for a valid token and valid matching password fields.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
path=/
authenticated_user=true
authenticated_admin=false
message=Your password has been changed successfully. You are now signed in.
password_changed=true

feature8_email_confirmation.json.description: Email confirmation requests and token consumption expose HTML/JSON framework outcomes and account confirmation state.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
path=/users/sign_in
message=You will receive an email with instructions for how to confirm your email address in a few minutes
mail_count=1
json_has_errors=false

feature9_account_unlock.json.description: Locked-account unlock requests and token consumption expose mail delivery, HTTP status, JSON errors, and lock state.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
path=/users/sign_in
message=You will receive an email with instructions for how to unlock your account in a few minutes
mail_count=1
json_has_errors=false
